Reply on 'Reference-only invocation doesn't load the committed config — what this means': When you run an agent, the invoke call can name the agent two ways.

- **By reference:** send only the app id. The service should look up that app's saved (committed) config and run it.
- **Inline:** send the whole config in the request body on every call.

On this deployment, the by-reference way does not work. If you send only the app id, the service ignores the saved config and runs a generic default agent instead (`gpt-5.5` / `pi_core`), not the agent you committed. The only way to actually run your agent is to resend its full config inline on every single call.

Analogy: you save a document, but every time you open it by its name you get a blank template. The only way to see your real document is to paste its full text in each time.

Why it matters: the playground and any real caller then have to carry and resend the entire config on every message. Pointing at "the committed agent" by id silently gives you the wrong agent, with no error. That is fragile and surprising. The `invoke-service` subagent is finding out whether this is a bug or an unfinished code path, and how the completion and chat services handle the same thing.

Reply on 'Multi-tool runs return an unreliable invoke output — explain more': When an agent uses several tools in one run, the reply you get back can lie about what happened.

Take the digest agent. It lists your Slack channels, reads GitHub issues, reads commits, then posts the digest to Slack. All four tool calls succeed. But the text the invoke returns comes back empty, or cut off mid-sentence, like "Now I'll post the digest to #social:". The work is done. The reply does not show it.

So the reply text is not a reliable sign of success. An empty reply does not mean the agent failed.

Two things cause this:

- **The run ends on the turn that calls the tools.** The agent does not always get a final turn to write "done, here is what I posted." So the service returns the last thing it said, even when that was half a sentence before the real work.
- **Sometimes the agent stops early.** It does the reads, then wanders off (into its own builtins, or "let me check the triggers"), and never reaches the last tool. That also returns no error. The job simply is not finished.

To know what really happened, read the trace, not the reply. The trace records every tool the run called and whether each one succeeded. The kit does this with `check-tools.sh`: give it the trace id and the tool that must run last, such as `SEND_MESSAGE`, and it prints `PASS` or `INCOMPLETE`.

Why this is a product gap: a caller should be able to trust the response. Today it cannot. You have to inspect spans to learn whether the job finished. That is both an observability problem and a contract problem, and it is worth fixing at the source, not just working around in the kit.

Reply on 'Kit's skill schema looks wrong (skills can have folders/files)': You were right, the kit was wrong. A skill (SkillTemplate, sdks/python/agenta/sdk/agents/skills/models.py:77) has SIX fields, not three: required name / description / body, plus optional files[] (each item {path, content, executable}), disable_model_invocation, and allow_executable_files. A folder is just /-separated segments in a file path (e.g. scripts/parse.py, references/schema.md) — there is no separate folder object; the runner materializes files[] into a directory beside SKILL.md. The [path,content] required set I saw earlier is the files[] item shape, not a second skill variant. name/description/body-only is valid but incomplete (that is how the platform builtins are built). The 500 the UC2 subagent hit was from putting slug/content at the TOP level; content belongs inside a files[] item. Skills can also be an @ag.embed reference the backend inlines. Full detail + a folder example in the findings file. Fixing BUILD-AGENT.md and verified-facts.md now.

Reply on 'Trace annotation: no agent-callable tool — did we not implement it yet?': Correct: it was never implemented as an agent-callable tool. PLATFORM_OPS (op_catalog.py:470) has 19 ops but no annotate; no gateway/builtin/MCP annotation tool exists. It is deferred and documented (build-notes.md, use-cases.md, status.md), not un-scoped — no PR yet. The interesting part: the run-context plumbing is already there and unused. RunContextTrace (dtos.py:440) carries the run own trace_id/span_id, populated per run from the OTel span (services/oss/src/agent/tracing.py:161), and its docstring literally reserves $ctx.trace.trace_id "for annotate my trace". So only the catalog entry is missing. Minimal shape: one PlatformOp(op=annotate_trace, POST /api/annotations/, args_into=annotation); the model supplies references.evaluator.slug + data.outputs, and the runner binds links.invocation.trace_id/span_id from $ctx.trace so the agent can only annotate its own current run (same self-targeting guarantee commit_revision uses). Default permission allow (open choice: ask, if evaluator auto-create is deemed sensitive). Full JSON-schema sketch in findings/annotation.md. This becomes a porting decision (it is a backend change).

Reply on 'Telegram: is there really no integration/trigger in Composio?': Half right. There IS a real Telegram integration in Composio for sending: telegram.SEND_MESSAGE is a genuine slug returned by Composio semantic search (adapter.py:211-284), and Agenta has NO integration allowlist — every integration is a live passthrough to Composio V3, so availability = whatever Composio returns. The needs_auth state was meaningful (real integration, OAuth, just not connected), not a phantom. BUT there is no Telegram incoming-message trigger on this deployment: Composio returned no telegram trigger event (corroborated by the manual test api/oss/tests/manual/triggers/try_composio_triggers.py, which notes Composio only exposes Slack messages-received). So a Telegram BOT (listen + reply) is NOT buildable here; a one-way send-to-Telegram agent would be, once Telegram is connected. Bonus finding worth acting on: find_triggers is KEYWORD scoring over the live catalog (service.py:330/463/170), not semantic search — "new telegram message" matched Slack SLACK_CHANNEL_MESSAGE_RECEIVED on the word "message", and candidate loaders even fall back to unfiltered catalog pages. A 2-term evidence guard exists (line 183) but the fall-through is the root of noisy matches. That is the same "discovery is approximate" issue and is fixable.

Reply on 'Reference-only invocation doesn't load the committed config — what this means': The why is now clear: this is a **bug**, not by design, and the fix is small.

The agent workflow seeds a **default config** into the SDK config registry: `agent=dict(v0=WorkflowRevisionData(parameters={"agent": build_agent_v0_default()}))` (`sdks/python/agenta/sdk/engines/running/utils.py:285-287`). Chat and completion seed an **empty** `WorkflowRevisionData()` instead.

That non-empty seed flips off the resolver's reference-hydration gate (`needs_reference_hydration`, `resolver.py:572-577`). So when you invoke by reference only, the service **skips** fetching your committed revision (`POST /applications/revisions/retrieve`) and runs the seeded default (`pi_core` / `gpt-5.5`). Chat and completion resolve references correctly precisely because they seed empty.

**Fix:** drop the `parameters` from the agent seed so it matches chat and completion. The handler already fills defaults from empty params, and `/inspect` still advertises the default schema. This is the single highest-value fix here: it removes the "inline the full config on every call" requirement entirely, so callers can point at a committed agent by id and actually get it.
Thread 'Reference-only invocation doesn't load the committed config — what this means': resolved

Reply on 'Agent service has no documented invoke contract — how do completion/chat solve it?': The answer to "how do completion and chat solve it" is: they do not. None of the three services expose OpenAPI.

`create_app` disables OpenAPI, Swagger, and ReDoc for every service by design (`sdks/python/agenta/sdk/decorators/routing.py:74-76`, and the outer app at `services/entrypoints/main.py:107-108`). So the 404 on `/services/agent/v0/openapi.json` is uniform, not an agent gap.

The real contract for all three today is **`POST .../inspect`**, which returns the inputs, parameters, and outputs schemas (agent schemas at `services/oss/src/agent/schemas.py:76-80`). That is the live, machine-readable contract right now.

To get proper OpenAPI: flip that shared default and enable `openapi_url` on the typed `invoke_endpoint(request: WorkflowInvokeRequest)` route. FastAPI would then generate the spec for agent, completion, and chat at once. So the fix is one shared change, not an agent-specific doc. Short term, point callers at `/inspect`.
Thread 'Agent service has no documented invoke contract — how do completion/chat solve it?': resolved

Reply on 'Invoke endpoint silently falls back instead of validating — how to fix': This is by-design looseness, not a targeted bug. Three things stack up:

- The request envelope models ignore unknown fields (Pydantic's default; `WorkflowRequestData` / `WorkflowInvokeRequest` at `sdks/python/agenta/sdk/models/workflows.py:237,296`).
- `parameters` is an unvalidated `Optional[dict]` (`workflows.py:239`).
- The agent-template parsing is best-effort with a per-field default fallback (`sdks/python/agenta/sdk/agents/dtos.py:1166-1204`).

So a misspelled field or wrong nesting is silently dropped and the run proceeds on defaults. The caller gets no 422 and no signal, which is what made the wrong-shape guessing so slow.

**Fix:** add strict validation scoped to the agent. Either return a 422 when `parameters` is non-empty but has no recognizable agent template, or validate `parameters.agent` against a strict model (`extra="forbid"`). The precedent already exists in the codebase: `WorkflowInvokeRequestFlags` uses `extra="forbid"` (`workflows.py:153`), as does `_SkillFileSchema`. Note this pairs naturally with the reference-only fix and the OpenAPI fix, since all three harden the same invoke route.
Thread 'Invoke endpoint silently falls back instead of validating — how to fix': resolved

Answered 'Fix the reference-only invoke bug (priority 1)': This is not a bug. An agent can be invoked either by a reference that includes a certain revision or by the whole configuration. If an agent wants to test something, it needs to invoke it. I am not using the configuration there, and I do not understand why you would invoke an agent like that. Where does this invocation happen?

The way you do it is that you create this configuration, and instead of committing it, if you want to test things, you run the agent with that configuration, just like the template does. That is how you do it if it is the same agent, whether you are editing it yourself or you have already created an agent and now want to edit it.

If you want to commit something and then test, you make the commit, and in the commit message you get the revision ID and the variant ID, and then you call it with that reference. I do not know if I am misunderstanding something. The behavior is that I do not understand what bug you are saying.

The bug is that it is running the default agent, even if you committed a revision. I think the way we do it is that if you call by app reference, it is the default variant, and then it calls the latest version of that. I do not understand what you mean by hydration or love. If you can provide more information, let's first make sure we are aligned on whether this is a bug or not, do the research we need to do to check that out, and then look at how to fix it. Is it a fixed behavior, maybe with some warnings, or actually a fix in the skill to tell the user how to use this correctly and tell the agent how to use it correctly?

Answered 'Harden the invoke route: OpenAPI + strict validation (priority 2)': Okay, so you say a strict validation in the parameter agents on invoke. I think that's fair. I think that's a good idea, but not on commit. If you invoke with parameters that don't work, we have some validation that could happen either when the parameters are in the URL request or when they are committed. We want a very clear warning or error explaining why that is the case.

Here you say a strict validation. Why would we limit the user from adding other stuff, especially on the outside? I just want to understand that. What's your plan there?

Enabling the OpenAPI URL, I don't think we should enable it. We have inspect that has the information. I don't know whether enabling it would actually get us the right schema, etc. If you can look into it and tell me what you think could happen there, that would be great. Let's add this in the instructions, including all these edge cases if they exist.

Answered 'Tighten find_triggers matching (lower priority)': Let's simply add it to the instructions. It's better to have high recall and high precision. Make sure it's clearly a search thing, and then we need to make sure it's the right thing. so, update the instructions there.

Reply on 'Reference-only invocation doesn't load the committed config — what this means': Good pushback. Let us align before touching anything.

Plain terms: "hydration" just means the service fetching your committed config for the reference you passed and filling it into the run. Nothing fancier.

Your model is right, and we actually agree on the definition. You invoke an agent two ways: (1) run with the full config inline while you are editing or testing, no commit, exactly like the template does; or (2) commit, get the revision id and variant id back, then invoke by that reference. The only open question is case (2): when you invoke by a committed reference with NO inline params, does the service run your committed config or the default agent? My lab test saw the default. You said that exact thing would be a bug ("running the default even if you committed a revision"). So we are aligned on the definition; we only need to confirm the behavior is real and find where it bites.

Where does this invocation happen (your question): that is the crux, so I am checking it. The two places that matter are the playground run and a trigger or schedule firing. If a schedule fires by reference and the service falls back to the default, then every scheduled agent runs the wrong config. That would be the real-world impact, and it is exactly what I want to confirm before calling it a bug.

Plan: (1) I re-test live now — commit an agent, invoke it by revision id and by app id with no inline params, and read the trace's resolved config. (2) A subagent maps where the product actually invokes by reference (playground, triggers, schedules) and confirms the code path. Then I report back: is it a real bug, and is the right fix code (make a reference load the committed config) or a doc/skill instruction (tell the user and the agent how to invoke correctly), or both with a clear warning. Keeping this decision open until we agree.

Reply on 'Invoke endpoint silently falls back instead of validating — how to fix': On your two questions.

Validation, and why not blanket "forbid": you are right to push on this. I am not proposing we reject arbitrary extra fields wholesale, and definitely not on commit. The goal is narrow: when `parameters.agent` is present but a field is misspelled or wrong-nested, the run should say so clearly instead of silently using defaults. So the plan is to validate the KNOWN agent-config fields and return a clear, specific error or warning that names the bad field and why, while still leaving room for extra metadata the user may want to attach. Where the check lives (on the invoke request vs at commit) is part of what I will confirm. The point is fail-loud with a good message, not lock the shape down.

OpenAPI: agreed, we should not just flip it on blind. A subagent is checking what enabling `openapi_url` would actually generate for the invoke route on this codebase — whether FastAPI produces a correct, useful schema, or a misleading one, and the risks of turning it on for all three services at once. Meanwhile `/inspect` stays the contract. I will report what enabling it would really do before recommending anything.

Either way, I will fold these invoke edge cases (inline config, /inspect is the contract, the validation behavior) into the kit instructions so the agent handles them correctly regardless of what we change at the source.

Reply on 'How to package + distribute Agenta skills (npx-style), where they live, CI': Research done (findings/skill-packaging.md). How npx skill installers work: an npm package with a bin that npx runs without install; it bundles the skill folder(s) and copies them into the target (default ./.claude/skills, --global to ~/.claude/skills). Versioning rides npm semver. Precedents: openskills, skills-claude, Vercel add-skill. Footgun: some write .agents/skills while Claude reads .claude/skills, so skills silently do not appear — target the right dir per harness. Native zero-npm alternative: Claude Code plugins + marketplaces (a .claude-plugin/marketplace.json in a git repo, installed via /plugin marketplace add agenta-ai/agenta) — zero infra, but Claude-only. Recommended repo home: a new top-level builder-kit/ (or skills/) with src/build-agenta-agent/ as the single canonical skill (SKILL.md + the kit scripts + references), an installer/ (package.json + bin/cli.js), and an optional .claude-plugin/. Keep it OUT of .agents/skills (contributor tooling) and out of sdks/ (Python). Apply the single-source PRINCIPLE (like agenta_builtins.py -> static_catalog.py) but a separate tree — this is a different product from the platform build-kit skills. CI/publish is greenfield: the repo publishes ZERO npm packages today (all @agenta/* are private), so this would be the first npm publish and needs an @agenta npm org + NPM_TOKEN. Recommended cheapest-first ladder: (a) commit marketplace.json -> /plugin (no infra); (b) npx giget gh:agenta-ai/agenta/... off a GitHub Release (no npm org); (c) full npx @agenta/skills add build-agent once the org exists. Ship (a)+(b) now, graduate to (c).

Reply on 'Claude and Agents.md': You were right, and it is a real bug with a small fix. Full trace in findings/agents-md-claude.md.

What happens: for the claude harness, `agents_md` is materialized ONLY as a file named `AGENTS.md` in the run's working directory (`workspace.ts:80`). It is never folded into the prompt, and the `systemPrompt`/`appendSystemPrompt` paths are gated behind `isPi`, so claude gets neither.

Why it is ignored: the bundled `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` memory loader auto-loads `CLAUDE.md` / `.claude/CLAUDE.md` / `CLAUDE.local.md` only. It never loads `AGENTS.md`. The adapter already sets `settingSources: [user, project, local]`, which WOULD load a `CLAUDE.md` if one existed, but the runner writes `AGENTS.md`, not `CLAUDE.md`. So the instructions are written to disk and then never read. Literally a filename mismatch.

That is the whole summarizer symptom: the agent never saw its "you are a summarizer" instructions, so a verb-less paste fell through to Claude Code's default coding-assistant persona ("can I help with a coding task?").

Fixes at the source:
- (A, smallest) for the claude harness, write `agents_md` to `CLAUDE.md` / `.claude/CLAUDE.md` instead of (or in addition to) `AGENTS.md`. `settingSources` already loads it, so it becomes "considered." One-file change in `workspace.ts`.
- (B, stronger persona) thread `agents_md` as a system-prompt append (`_meta.systemPrompt.append`). The Zed adapter honors it, but sandbox-agent 0.4.2 strips `_meta` from session init, so it must first expose that hook. Bigger change.
- (C) both, mirroring how Pi already splits an AGENTS.md preamble plus an append_system persona.

Kit workaround we already use: frame the task with an explicit verb ("Summarize the following text:"). The coding persona still executes an explicit instruction; only bare pastes fall through. But that is a workaround, not the fix.

Reply on 'Reference-only invocation doesn't load the committed config — what this means': Corrected verdict: this is NOT a production bug, and your pushback was right.

What actually happens across the real callers:
- Playground (agent chat) always sends the full config inline, so it runs your committed/edited config.
- Triggers and schedules fire by reference only, BUT every invoke goes through the API's `_ensure_request_revision` (`api/oss/src/core/workflows/service.py`), which fetches the committed revision server-side and writes it into `request.data.revision` before forwarding to the agent service. The SDK resolver reads `data.revision` before the seeded default, so the committed config runs. So a scheduled agent does NOT run the wrong config. My earlier investigation missed this API-side hydration layer.
- The ONLY path that falls back to the default (pi_core/gpt-5.5) is a direct reference-only call to the low-level agent service endpoint that bypasses the API. That is exactly what my lab test did (I called {host}/services/agent/v0/invoke directly with references and no params). That is not how the product invokes.

So your model was correct: inline while editing, or commit and invoke by reference, and it works. I was testing the wrong door.

There is one real but latent inconsistency worth knowing: the agent is the only builtin workflow whose registry seeds a non-empty default config, and that non-empty seed disables the SDK's own reference-hydration net. Completion and chat seed empty, so if THEY are called reference-only at the service level they hydrate correctly; the agent does not. It has no production impact today (nothing calls the agent service reference-only), but if we ever want the agent service to behave like completion/chat for a direct reference call, the fix is one line: seed the agent registry empty like the others. Low priority, your call.

- **Marketplace, point at a GitHub repo (no installer code):** this is the Claude Code plugin/marketplace model. You commit a `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` in the Agenta repo listing the skill(s). A user runs `/plugin marketplace add agenta-ai/agenta` then `/plugin install build-agent`, and it pulls the skill straight from the repo. Zero npm, zero installer code, versioned by git. This matches exactly what you described, and it is the right first step.
- **npx installer (writing code):** this is the heavier, harness-agnostic option. You would only reach for it to also cover Codex and Cursor (which read `.agents/skills`, not `.claude/skills`), or for a branded `npx @agenta/skills` one-liner. It is not required to ship.

On "our Open Skills": `openskills` is an existing community npx installer. So even for the npx route we would REUSE it rather than write our own installer code (`npx openskills add <repo>/<skill>`), which removes the "write the code" surprise. But you do not need it at all if we go marketplace-first.

Recommendation: ship the marketplace-from-repo path first (one `marketplace.json` committed to the repo, no code), and only add an npx path later if we want Codex/Cursor coverage. If you want, I will draft the `marketplace.json` and the repo layout so the skill installs by pointing at the GitHub repo, and you review it.

Reply on 'Reference-only invocation doesn't load the committed config — what this means': Settled, with live proof, and it turns out my failed tests were a payload-shape error on my side.

The working invoke that runs your committed config (proven live: RESOLVED = harness=claude, model=sonnet, 200, trace cffc952469f05806637356aff0c8ec71): send the revision DOUBLE-nested.

```
data.revision = { "data": <application_revision.data> }
```

where `<application_revision.data>` is exactly what `POST /api/applications/revisions/retrieve` returns.

Why my earlier attempts failed:
- Attempt 1 sent references only (no revision). The service does NOT self-hydrate a bare reference, so it ran the seeded default (gpt-5.5) and 500'd.
- Attempt 2 sent `data.revision = <revision.data>` SINGLE-nested. The resolver reads `data.revision["data"]` (`resolver.py:150`), so a single-nested dump has no inner `data` key and is skipped, falling through to the default again. I had the nesting one level off.

Verdict, per path:
- **Product (playground, triggers, HITL): NOT a bug.** The API's `_ensure_request_revision` pre-hydrates the reference into the double-nested `data.revision` before forwarding, so your committed config runs. Every product caller does this. Proven live.
- **Raw reference-only call directly to the service endpoint: a real gap.** The service does not hydrate a bare reference itself; it relies on the API to pre-hydrate. A references-only POST to `/services/agent/v0/invoke` runs the default. Low priority (no product caller does this), but if we want the service to self-hydrate a bare reference like completion and chat do, that is the one-line "seed the agent registry empty" fix.
- **My earlier "not a bug": half right.** Production is fine, but my stated mechanism was wrong, and the real reason my replication failed was double-vs-single nesting. Sorry for the churn.

Reply on 'Multi-tool runs return an unreliable invoke output — explain more': Confirmed live, your hypothesis was right. Streaming invoke (same endpoint, Accept: application/x-ndjson) returns EVERY event. Side-by-side on the 4-tool digest agent: batch came back as one mid-sentence 105-char message with no tools shown (the bug); streaming returned 48 events — all 4 tool calls in order, their results, and the full digest carried in a user_approval interaction, then done. And I filed a design PR #5003: the batch empty-output is a coalescing choice, not a limitation (the result already carries all messages/events; batch just returns the final text), so the fix is to have batch coalesce the whole turn into outputs.messages.
